content:
# Leviticus Chapter 26 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 74r–74v
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 112–113
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_026.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 26 is the theological crown of the Holiness Code and one of the most architecturally deliberate chapters in the entire Torah. It functions as a formal covenant document in the ancient Near Eastern suzerain-vassal treaty tradition: an opening prologue of two cardinal commands (vv. 1–2), a blessings section for obedience (vv. 3–13), a five-stage escalating curse sequence for disobedience (vv. 14–39), and a remarkable closing section of grace in which God commits to covenant memory even through exile (vv. 40–45), followed by a legal colophon (v. 46).

The structural genius of the chapter lies in its asymmetry: eleven verses of blessing culminate in the summit of verse 12 — "I will walk among you and be your God, and you shall be my people" — followed by twenty-five verses of escalating catastrophe that are nonetheless unable to reach the floor established in verse 44: "I will not spurn them, neither will I abhor them so as to destroy them utterly and break my covenant with them." The curse cannot be the last word; God put the floor in himself, in writing.

The five curse stages are each triggered by the conditional "if you will not listen to me" (vv. 14, 18, 21, 23, 27) and each amplified by the sevenfold intensification formula (vv. 18, 21, 24, 28). The key covenant term **qeri** ("contrary, in opposition") appears seven times in the chapter (vv. 21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 40, 41), forming the spine of the curse's legal logic: YHWH himself walks "contrary" to Israel when Israel walks "contrary" to YHWH. The covenant is bilateral — and so is its breakdown.

Verse 12 is the theological apex: *ve-hithalakhti be-tokheikhem* — the Hithpael of *halakh*, "I will walk about among you," the same reflexive-intensive form used of God walking in Eden (Gen 3:8) and of Enoch and Noah who "walked with God" (Gen 5:22; 6:9). All of Leviticus — its blood offerings, purity laws, calendar observances, and ethical demands — is scaffolding in service of this single promise: God dwelling and walking in the midst of his people.

**Key term rulings:** YHWH = "the LORD" | bney yisrael = "sons of Israel" | qeri = "contrary" | chuqqot/chuqqim = "statutes" | mishpatim = "rules/ordinances" | torot = "laws" | berith = "covenant" | hesed (v. 43) = "rules" (as mishpatim context) | nefesh = "soul" (in fixed formulae) | massebah = "pillar" | even maskiyit = "figured stone" | elilim = "idols" | pesel = "carved image" | bamot = "high places" | chamanim = "incense altars" | gilulim = "idols/dung-idols" | sheva = "sevenfold" | qomemiyut = "erect" (walk erect)

---

## Consensus Translation

**26:1** "You shall not make idols for yourselves, nor shall you erect a carved image or a pillar for yourselves, nor shall you set up a figured stone in your land to bow down to it, for I am the LORD your God.

**26:2** You shall keep my Sabbaths and reverence my sanctuary: I am the LORD.

**26:3** "If you walk in my statutes and observe my commandments and do them,

**26:4** then I will give your rains in their season, and the land shall yield its produce, and the trees of the field shall yield their fruit.

**26:5** Your threshing shall last to the time of the grape harvest, and the grape harvest shall last to the time of sowing. And you shall eat your bread to the full and dwell in your land securely.

**26:6** I will give peace in the land, and you shall lie down, and none shall make you afraid. And I will remove harmful beasts from the land, and the sword shall not pass through your land.

**26:7** You shall chase your enemies, and they shall fall before you by the sword.

**26:8** Five of you shall chase a hundred, and a hundred of you shall chase ten thousand, and your enemies shall fall before you by the sword.

**26:9** I will turn to you and make you fruitful and multiply you and will confirm my covenant with you.

**26:10** You shall eat old grain long kept, and you shall clear out the old to make way for the new.

**26:11** I will make my dwelling among you, and my soul shall not abhor you.

**26:12** And I will walk among you and will be your God, and you shall be my people.

**26:13** I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, that you should not be their slaves. And I have broken the bars of your yoke and made you walk erect.

**26:14** "But if you will not listen to me and will not do all these commandments,

**26:15** if you spurn my statutes, and if your soul abhors my rules, so that you will not do all my commandments, and so break my covenant,

**26:16** then I will do this to you: I will visit you with panic, with wasting disease and fever that consume the eyes and make the heart ache. And you shall sow your seed in vain, for your enemies shall eat it.

**26:17** I will set my face against you, and you shall be struck down before your enemies. Those who hate you shall rule over you, and you shall flee when none pursues you.

**26:18** And if in spite of this you will not listen to me, then I will discipline you again sevenfold for your sins,

**26:19** and I will break the pride of your power, and I will make your heavens like iron and your earth like bronze.

**26:20** And your strength shall be spent in vain, for your land shall not yield its produce, and the trees of the land shall not yield their fruit.

**26:21** "Then if you walk contrary to me and will not listen to me, I will continue striking you sevenfold for your sins.

**26:22** And I will let loose the wild beasts against you, which shall bereave you of your children and destroy your livestock and make you few in number, so that your roads shall be deserted.

**26:23** "And if by these things you are not turned to me but walk contrary to me,

**26:24** then I also will walk contrary to you, and I myself will strike you sevenfold for your sins.

**26:25** And I will bring a sword upon you, that shall execute vengeance for the covenant. And if you gather within your cities, I will send pestilence among you, and you shall be delivered into the hand of the enemy.

**26:26** When I break your supply of bread, ten women shall bake your bread in a single oven and shall dole out your bread again by weight, and you shall eat and not be satisfied.

**26:27** "But if in spite of this you will not listen to me, but walk contrary to me,

**26:28** then I will walk contrary to you in fury, and I myself will discipline you sevenfold for your sins.

**26:29** You shall eat the flesh of your sons, and you shall eat the flesh of your daughters.

**26:30** And I will destroy your high places and cut down your incense altars and cast your dead bodies upon the dead bodies of your idols, and my soul shall abhor you.

**26:31** And I will lay your cities waste and will make your sanctuaries desolate, and I will not smell your pleasing aromas.

**26:32** And I myself will devastate the land, so that your enemies who settle in it shall be appalled at it.

**26:33** And I will scatter you among the nations, and I will unsheathe the sword after you, and your land shall be a desolation, and your cities shall be a waste.

**26:34** "Then the land shall enjoy its Sabbaths as long as it lies desolate, while you are in your enemies' land; then the land shall rest and enjoy its Sabbaths.

**26:35** As long as it lies desolate it shall have rest, the rest that it did not have on your Sabbaths when you were dwelling in it.

**26:36** And as for those of you who are left, I will send faintness into their hearts in the lands of their enemies. The sound of a driven leaf shall put them to flight, and they shall flee as one flees from the sword, and they shall fall when none pursues.

**26:37** They shall stumble over one another, as if to escape a sword, though none pursues. And you shall have no power to stand before your enemies.

**26:38** And you shall perish among the nations, and the land of your enemies shall eat you up.

**26:39** And those of you who are left shall rot away in your enemies' lands because of their iniquity, and also because of the iniquities of their fathers they shall rot away like them.

**26:40** "But if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery that they committed against me, and also in walking contrary to me,

**26:41** so that I walked contrary to them and brought them into the land of their enemies — if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled and they make amends for their iniquity,

**26:42** then I will remember my covenant with Jacob, and I will remember my covenant with Isaac and my covenant with Abraham, and I will remember the land.

**26:43** But the land shall be abandoned by them and enjoy its Sabbaths while it lies desolate without them, and they shall make amends for their iniquity, because they spurned my rules and their soul abhorred my statutes.

**26:44** Yet for all that, when they are in the land of their enemies, I will not spurn them, neither will I abhor them so as to destroy them utterly and break my covenant with them, for I am the LORD their God.

**26:45** But I will for their sake remember the covenant with their forefathers, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt in the sight of the nations, that I might be their God: I am the LORD."

**26:46** These are the statutes and rules and laws that the LORD made between himself and the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai through Moses.

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — "Make idols" vs. "make for yourselves idols."** EMET: "You shall not make for yourselves elilim [idols]." LOGOS: "You shall not make for yourselves idols, nor shall you erect a carved image or a pillar for yourselves." PNEUMA: "Do not make idols for yourselves. Do not set up a carved image or a sacred stone." Consensus follows LOGOS for formal structure and "for yourselves" placement. PNEUMA breaks the verse into three short imperative sentences — smoother but loses the unified prohibition pattern of the MT. EMET preserves transliterated *elilim*; consensus renders "idols" throughout.

**V. 1 — "Sacred stone" vs. "pillar" for massebah.** PNEUMA: "sacred stone." EMET and LOGOS: "pillar." Per project convention (Gen 28; Lev 19 precedent), massebah is rendered "pillar" in the consensus. PNEUMA's "sacred stone" is interpretively accurate but differs from established project convention.

**V. 1 — "Figured stone" vs. "sculpted stone."** EMET and LOGOS: "figured stone." PNEUMA: "sculpted stone." Consensus: "figured stone" — the established formal translation (ESV, NASB) for *even maskiyit*, and consistent with EMET/LOGOS agreement.

**V. 2 — "Reverence" vs. "stand in awe of" for tira'u.** LOGOS: "reverence my sanctuary." EMET: "fear/reverence my sanctuary." PNEUMA: "stand in awe of my sanctuary." Consensus: "reverence my sanctuary" — LOGOS. All three convey the *yir'ah* ("fear/awe") rightly; "reverence" is more formal and appropriate for a legal covenant text.

**V. 3 — "Observe" vs. "keep" vs. "carry out" for shomer/asah.** LOGOS: "walk in my statutes and observe my commandments and do them." PNEUMA: "walk in my statutes and keep my commandments and carry them out." EMET: "walk in my statutes and keep my commandments and do them." Consensus: LOGOS's "observe... and do them" — slight preference over "keep... and do" (EMET) or "keep... and carry them out" (PNEUMA) for rhythm and formal register. The Hebrew has *tishmeru* (keep/guard/observe) and *asitem* (do them).

**V. 4 — "Give your rains" vs. "send rain."** LOGOS: "I will give your rains in their season." EMET: "I will give your rains in their time." PNEUMA: "I will send rain for your land at the right time — the autumn rains and the spring rains." Consensus: LOGOS — "I will give your rains in their season." PNEUMA adds "the autumn rains and the spring rains" — an interpretive expansion not in the MT's plain text here. "Season" preferred over EMET's "time" for *be'itam*.

**V. 5 — "Eat your bread to the full" vs. "eat your fill of food."** LOGOS: "eat your bread to the full." PNEUMA: "eat your fill of food." EMET: "eat your bread to satisfaction." Consensus: "eat your bread to the full" — LOGOS. The MT has *lechem* (bread/food) specifically; "food" (PNEUMA) is vaguer than the Hebrew noun.

**V. 6 — "Harmful beasts" vs. "dangerous animals" vs. "evil beasts."** LOGOS: "harmful beasts." PNEUMA: "dangerous animals." EMET: "evil beasts [chayah ra'ah]." Consensus: "harmful beasts" — LOGOS. "Harmful" better represents *ra'ah* in this context than PNEUMA's "dangerous" (a modern safety register), and is more idiomatic than EMET's literal "evil beasts."

**V. 6 — "Pass through" vs. "go through."** LOGOS: "shall not go through your land." EMET: "shall not pass through your land." PNEUMA: "will not pass through your country." Consensus: "shall not pass through your land" — EMET/PNEUMA. LOGOS's "go through" is slightly weaker than the Hebrew *ta'avor* ("pass over/through"). "Pass through" is the more established rendering.

**V. 9 — "Turn to you" vs. "I will turn toward you."** LOGOS: "I will turn to you." PNEUMA: "I will turn toward you." EMET: "I will face toward you [paniti aleikhem]." Consensus: "I will turn to you" — LOGOS. The Hebrew *paniti aleikhem* is the Qal of *panah* ("to turn") + prepositional phrase; "turn to you" is slightly more compact than "turn toward you" (PNEUMA) with no loss of meaning.

**V. 11 — "Make my dwelling" vs. "place my tabernacle."** LOGOS: "I will make my dwelling among you." EMET: "I will give my dwelling-place [mishkani] in your midst." PNEUMA: "I will make my dwelling among you." Consensus: "I will make my dwelling among you" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's "give my dwelling-place" is literal for *ve-natati mishkani* but "make my dwelling" is more idiomatic. "Among you" vs. EMET's "in your midst" — both are valid for *be-tokheikhem*; "among you" is preferred for the consensus as cleaner.

**V. 13 — "Walk erect" vs. "walk with your heads held high."** LOGOS: "made you walk erect." EMET: "made you walk erect [qomemiyut]." PNEUMA: "made you walk with your heads held high." Consensus: "made you walk erect" — EMET/LOGOS. *Qomemiyut* is a hapax meaning upright posture/stature; "walk erect" is the most concise formal rendering. PNEUMA's "heads held high" is an evocative paraphrase but adds interpretive specificity beyond the text.

**V. 13 — "From the house of slavery."** PNEUMA adds "from the house of slavery" to the Exodus formula. EMET and LOGOS do not include this phrase in v. 13. The MT of 26:13 reads simply *me-eretz Mitzraim me-heyot lahem avadim* ("from the land of Egypt from being their slaves") — "house of slavery" (*beit avadim*) is the Exodus/Deuteronomy formula (Exod 20:2; Deut 5:6) but is not in the MT of Lev 26:13. Consensus follows MT: "from the land of Egypt, that you should not be their slaves."

**V. 15 — "Spurn" vs. "reject" for ma'as.** LOGOS: "if you spurn my statutes." EMET: "if you reject my statutes [tima'asu]." PNEUMA: "if you reject my statutes." Consensus: "if you spurn my statutes" — LOGOS. "Spurn" carries a slightly stronger contemptuous force than "reject," matching the semantic weight of *ma'as* (to despise, reject with contempt). The same root appears in the positive use at v. 44 ("I will not spurn them") — using "spurn" for both occurrences creates important verbal echo. LOGOS uses "spurn" for *ma'as* consistently.

**V. 15 — "Abhors my rules" vs. "turns away from my laws."** LOGOS: "your soul abhors my rules." PNEUMA: "your soul turns away from my laws." EMET: "your soul loathes [tigh'al nafshekhem] my ordinances." Consensus: "your soul abhors my rules" — LOGOS. *Tigh'al* (from *ga'al*, "to abhor/reject with disgust") is a strong emotional-physical revulsion term; "abhors" captures this better than PNEUMA's "turns away from." EMET's "loathes" is also close but "abhors" has a slightly more elevated register suited to the legal text.

**V. 16 — "Visit you with panic" vs. "bring sudden terror."** LOGOS: "I will visit you with panic." EMET: "I will appoint over you [ve-hikdashti aleikhem] panic [behalah]." PNEUMA: "sudden terror, wasting disease, and burning fever." Consensus: "I will visit you with panic" — LOGOS. "Visit with" for *pakad* is the standard covenantal-legal rendering; PNEUMA's restructuring makes "sudden terror" coordinate with the diseases, whereas the MT introduces *behalah* separately as a first blow.

**V. 16 — "Make the heart ache" vs. "drains your life away."** LOGOS: "make the heart ache." EMET: "making the soul pine away [medevot nefesh]." PNEUMA: "drains your life away." Consensus: "make the heart ache" — LOGOS. The Hebrew *medevot nefesh* ("causing the soul/heart to pine/languish") is a slow-grief image; "make the heart ache" (LOGOS) best captures the Hebrew idiom in formal English. PNEUMA's "drains your life away" is dynamic but shifts from emotional languishing to physical depletion.

**V. 17 — "Struck down before your enemies" vs. "defeated."** All three agents agree on the sense. LOGOS: "you shall be struck down before your enemies." EMET: "you shall be smitten before your enemies." PNEUMA: "you will be struck down before your enemies." Consensus: "you shall be struck down before your enemies" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. "Struck down" over EMET's more archaic "smitten."

**V. 18 — "Discipline you again sevenfold" vs. "punish you seven times over."** LOGOS: "I will discipline you again sevenfold for your sins." EMET: "I will add to discipline you seven-times over for your sins." PNEUMA: "I will go on to punish you seven times over for your sins." Consensus: "I will discipline you again sevenfold for your sins" — LOGOS. "Discipline" for *yasar* (Hiphil, "to chastise/discipline") is more precise than PNEUMA's "punish" — *yasar* carries the sense of corrective discipline, not merely punitive retribution. "Sevenfold" is preferred over "seven times over" as a single compound adverb that preserves the ritual-number force of *sheva*.

**V. 19 — "Pride of your power" vs. "pride you take in your own strength."** LOGOS: "the pride of your power." PNEUMA: "the pride you take in your own strength." EMET: "the pride of your strength [ge'on uzekhem]." Consensus: "the pride of your power" — LOGOS. *Ge'on oz* = "pride of strength/power"; "power" is a suitable rendering for *oz* and "pride" for *ga'on*. PNEUMA's "the pride you take in your own strength" is more expansive and interpretive.

**V. 21 — "Walk contrary to me" for qeri.** LOGOS: "if you walk contrary to me." EMET: "if you walk contrary to me [be-qeri]." PNEUMA: "If you continue to oppose me." Consensus: "if you walk contrary to me" — EMET/LOGOS. The *qeri* term appears seven times in this chapter and its verbal form ("walk contrary to") must be rendered consistently across all occurrences (vv. 21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 40, 41). PNEUMA renders variously ("oppose me," "continue to oppose me") — this loses the chapter's distinctive terminological spine. "Walk contrary to me" is the standard formal rendering (ESV) and is adopted throughout.

**V. 22 — "Let loose the wild beasts" vs. "send wild animals."** LOGOS: "I will let loose the wild beasts against you." EMET: "I will send against you the wild beasts of the field." PNEUMA: "I will send wild animals among you." Consensus: "I will let loose the wild beasts against you" — LOGOS. "Let loose" for *shilachti* (Piel of *shalach*, "to send/release") conveys the unleashing of uncontrolled force; "send" (EMET/PNEUMA) is technically accurate but less vivid. "Wild beasts" for *chayat ha-sadeh* over PNEUMA's "wild animals" — "beasts" has a stronger force matching the context of children being bereaved.

**V. 22 — "Bereave you of your children."** LOGOS: "shall bereave you of your children." EMET: "that will bereave you of your children." PNEUMA: "they will rob you of your children." Consensus: "shall bereave you of your children" — LOGOS/EMET. "Bereave" for *shikkel* (Piel, "to make childless/bereave") is the precise term for a parent losing children; PNEUMA's "rob you of" loses the specific bereavement force of *shikkel*.

**V. 23 — "By these things you are not turned" vs. "after all this discipline."** LOGOS: "if by these things you are not turned to me." EMET: "if by these [things] you will not be corrected [lo tivaseru]." PNEUMA: "If after all this discipline you still do not turn back to me." Consensus: "if by these things you are not turned to me" — LOGOS. EMET's "corrected" is from *yasar*; LOGOS's "turned" captures the *lo tivaseru* (Niphal of *yasar*, "be disciplined/corrected") while emphasizing the turning-toward-God dimension. PNEUMA's paraphrase adds "after all this discipline" which is interpretive expansion.

**V. 25 — "Execute vengeance for the covenant."** LOGOS: "that shall execute vengeance for the covenant." EMET: "executing the vengeance of the covenant [niqmat berit]." PNEUMA: "to execute the vengeance of the covenant." Consensus: "that shall execute vengeance for the covenant" — LOGOS. The phrase *niqmat berit* ("vengeance/retribution of the covenant") is a legal formula; LOGOS's relative clause "that shall execute" is the most natural English structure. PNEUMA uses the same formula ("vengeance of the covenant") but LOGOS's "for the covenant" is slightly more idiomatic.

**V. 26 — "Break your supply of bread" vs. "cut off your supply of bread."** LOGOS: "When I break your supply of bread." EMET: "In my breaking to you the staff of bread [mateh lechem]." PNEUMA: "When I cut off your supply of bread." Consensus: "When I break your supply of bread" — LOGOS. The Hebrew *be-shivri lakem mateh lechem* = "when I break for you the staff of bread" — the "staff of bread" (*mateh lechem*) is a metaphor for the food-supply support system. LOGOS's "your supply of bread" is an acceptable interpretation of the idiomatic *mateh*; EMET's "staff of bread" is literal but requires a gloss; PNEUMA's "cut off" changes the verb from *shavar* (break) to cutting, losing the specific image.

**V. 28 — "Walk contrary to you in fury."** LOGOS: "I will walk contrary to you in fury." EMET: "I will walk contrary [ve-halakhti imi be-qeri chamat] — in fury-heat." PNEUMA: "in my burning anger I will oppose you." Consensus: "I will walk contrary to you in fury" — LOGOS. *Chamat qeri* = "in the wrath/fury of contrariness" — LOGOS's "in fury" preserves both elements compactly. PNEUMA's "in my burning anger" removes the *qeri* ("contrary") dimension, treating it as pure anger rather than the specific covenantal-counter-walking that *qeri* signifies.

**V. 29 — "Eat the flesh of your sons and daughters."** All three agree. LOGOS: "You shall eat the flesh of your sons, and you shall eat the flesh of your daughters." EMET: same structure. PNEUMA: "You will eat the flesh of your own sons and daughters." Consensus: LOGOS. The MT repeats "you shall eat the flesh of" for both sons and daughters; LOGOS preserves this repetition. PNEUMA merges it ("sons and daughters") and adds "own" — the MT does not have "own."

**V. 30 — "Cast your dead bodies upon the dead bodies of your idols."** LOGOS: "cast your dead bodies upon the dead bodies of your idols." EMET: "I will cast your corpses [pigreikhem] upon the corpses [pigrei] of your dung-idols [giluleikhem]." PNEUMA: "I will pile your corpses on the corpses of your idols." Consensus: LOGOS with "cast." "Dead bodies" preferred over EMET's "corpses" for somewhat elevated register; "cast" preferred over PNEUMA's "pile" as closer to *natati* ("I will give/place/cast"). "Idols" preferred over EMET's "dung-idols" for the consensus text (the pejorative *gilulim* is noted in the commentary but "idols" is the conventional formal rendering).

**V. 31 — "Lay your cities waste" vs. "demolish your cities."** LOGOS: "I will lay your cities waste." PNEUMA: "I will demolish your cities." EMET: "I will make your cities a waste [lecharavah]." Consensus: "I will lay your cities waste" — LOGOS. "Lay waste" for *natatim lechorban* is the formal idiom; "demolish" (PNEUMA) is more physical and less covenantally resonant.

**V. 31 — "I will not smell your pleasing aromas."** LOGOS: "I will not smell your pleasing aromas." EMET: "I will not smell your aroma of soothing [reiach nikhokheikhem]." PNEUMA: "I will not inhale the soothing fragrance of your offerings." Consensus: "I will not smell your pleasing aromas" — LOGOS. "Pleasing aromas" for *reiach nikhokh* (lit. "aroma of restfulness/soothing") is the standard Levitical sacrifice formula throughout the book (Lev 1:9, 13, 17, etc.) — maintaining this fixed rendering in v. 31 creates the intended contrast with all the earlier sacrifice acceptances. PNEUMA's "soothing fragrance of your offerings" adds "offerings" which is not in the MT text here.

**V. 32 — "Shall be appalled at it" vs. "will be appalled by it."** LOGOS: "shall be appalled at it." PNEUMA: "will be appalled by it." EMET: "shall be appalled over her." Consensus: "shall be appalled at it" — LOGOS. Minor preposition variation; "at" is slightly more natural in English. EMET's "over her" (feminine pronoun for the land) is literal but awkward.

**V. 33 — "Unsheathe the sword after you."** LOGOS: "I will unsheathe the sword after you." EMET: "I will draw out [hariqoti] the sword behind you." PNEUMA: "draw out the sword to pursue you." Consensus: "I will unsheathe the sword after you" — LOGOS. "Unsheathe" for *hariq* (Hiphil of *riq*, "to empty/draw out") is vivid and precise. PNEUMA's "to pursue you" interprets the "after you" directional, but the MT's *achareikhhem* is more idiomatically rendered "after you." The sword "after" the fleeing exiles is a more haunting image than "to pursue you."

**V. 34 — "Enjoy its Sabbaths" for tirzeh shabetoteha.** LOGOS: "the land shall enjoy its Sabbaths." EMET: "the land shall be satisfied with its Sabbaths [tirzeh et-shabetoteha]." PNEUMA: "the land will enjoy its Sabbaths." Consensus: "the land shall enjoy its Sabbaths" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The verb *ratzah* ("to be pleased with, to find satisfaction in") is rendered "enjoy" — capturing the satisfaction dimension without the slightly awkward "be satisfied with" (EMET). This rendering applies across all three occurrences of the formula (vv. 34, 35, 43).

**V. 36 — "Sound of a driven leaf" vs. "sound of a wind-driven leaf."** LOGOS: "The sound of a driven leaf shall put them to flight." EMET: "the sound of a driven leaf shall pursue them." PNEUMA: "the sound of a wind-driven leaf will send them fleeing." Consensus: "The sound of a driven leaf shall put them to flight" — LOGOS. The MT has *qol aleh niddaf* ("the sound of a driven/tossed leaf") without specifying wind explicitly; PNEUMA's "wind-driven" adds specificity. "Put them to flight" over EMET's "pursue them" — the leaf does not pursue; it causes flight. LOGOS is more natural.

**V. 39 — "Rot away" for yimmaqqu.** LOGOS: "shall rot away in your enemies' lands." EMET: "shall melt away [yimmaqqu] in their iniquity." PNEUMA: "will waste away in the lands of their enemies." Consensus: "shall rot away in your enemies' lands" — LOGOS. *Maqaq* (Niphal, "to waste/rot/dissolve away") is a slow-decay term; "rot away" (LOGOS) captures the organic dissolution better than PNEUMA's "waste away" (more general) or EMET's "melt away" (suggests liquid dissolution rather than organic decay).

**V. 40 — "Treachery" for ma'al.** LOGOS: "their treachery that they committed against me." EMET: "their act of treachery [ma'alam] which they committed against me." PNEUMA: "their treachery against me." Consensus: "their treachery that they committed against me" — LOGOS. *Ma'al* = breach of faith, treachery, covenant violation; "treachery" is the established rendering (ESV, NASB).

**V. 41 — "Uncircumcised heart is humbled."** LOGOS: "their uncircumcised heart is humbled." EMET: "their uncircumcised heart [levavam he'arel] is humbled." PNEUMA: "their uncircumcised hearts are humbled." Consensus: "their uncircumcised heart is humbled" — LOGOS/EMET (singular). The MT has the singular *levavam* ("their heart" — collective singular); the plural "hearts" (PNEUMA) is grammatically acceptable in English but the singular is more faithful to the Hebrew and preserves the collective unity of the repentant community.

**V. 41 — "Make amends for their iniquity" for yirtzu et-avonam.** LOGOS: "make amends for their iniquity." EMET: "accept/bear their iniquity [yirtzu et-avonam]." PNEUMA: "accept the punishment for their iniquity." Consensus: "make amends for their iniquity" — LOGOS. The Hebrew *ratzah* here (as in vv. 34–35 for the land's Sabbaths) means "to be satisfied with, to bear willingly, to make payment for." LOGOS's "make amends" captures the penal-satisfaction sense well. PNEUMA's "accept the punishment" is interpretive; EMET's "accept/bear their iniquity" is more literal.

**V. 42 — Reverse patriarchal order Jacob–Isaac–Abraham.** All three agents preserve the reversed order. LOGOS: "my covenant with Jacob... with Isaac... with Abraham." PNEUMA: same. Consensus: "my covenant with Jacob, and I will remember my covenant with Isaac and my covenant with Abraham, and I will remember the land" — LOGOS. The repeated "I will remember" before each patriarchal name is in the MT and is preserved (EMET and LOGOS keep all three; PNEUMA collapses slightly). The reverse chronological order (Jacob–Isaac–Abraham) is a deliberate rhetorical movement from most recent to most foundational, walking back to the unconditional Abrahamic promise.

**V. 44 — "Spurn... abhor" for ma'as / ga'al.** LOGOS: "I will not spurn them, neither will I abhor them." EMET: "I will not reject them and I will not abhor them." PNEUMA: "I will not reject them or turn away from them." Consensus: "I will not spurn them, neither will I abhor them" — LOGOS. "Spurn" for *ma'as* is preferred (established at v. 15); "abhor" for *ga'al* — same verb used in v. 11 ("my soul shall not abhor you") and v. 30 ("my soul shall abhor you") — the verbal echo across the chapter is theologically significant and must be preserved. PNEUMA's "turn away from them" for *ga'al* loses the specific abhorrence vocabulary.

**V. 44 — "So as to destroy them utterly."** LOGOS: "so as to destroy them utterly." EMET: "to destroy them completely [le-khalotam]." PNEUMA: "so as to destroy them utterly." Consensus: "so as to destroy them utterly" — LOGOS/PNEUMA.

**V. 45 — "Covenant with their forefathers" for berit rishonim.** LOGOS: "the covenant with their forefathers." EMET: "the covenant of the first ones [berit rishonim]." PNEUMA: "the covenant with the ancestors." Consensus: "the covenant with their forefathers" — LOGOS. *Rishonim* ("the former ones / first ones") here denotes the Exodus generation (the Sinaitic covenant partners), not specifically the patriarchs of v. 42. "Forefathers" (LOGOS) captures the ancestral-covenant-generation sense well; "ancestors" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but somewhat generic; "first ones" (EMET) is accurate but unusual English.

**V. 46 — "Statutes and rules and laws" for chuqqim, mishpatim, torot.** LOGOS: "statutes and rules and laws." EMET: "statutes and ordinances and laws [ve-ha-torot]." PNEUMA: "statutes and the judgments and the laws." Consensus: "statutes and rules and laws" — LOGOS. Three distinct Hebrew terms: *chuqqim* (statutes), *mishpatim* (rules/judgments), *torot* (laws/teachings — plural of Torah). LOGOS uses the same rendering established for these terms throughout Leviticus. PNEUMA's "judgments" for *mishpatim* is the older formal rendering; "rules" (LOGOS/ESV) is the modern formal consensus and has been the project standard.

**V. 46 — "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites."** LOGOS: "sons of Israel." EMET: "sons of Israel." PNEUMA: "Israelites." Consensus: "sons of Israel" — per project convention, *bney yisrael* is consistently rendered "sons of Israel" throughout the consensus translation.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On verse 12 as theological apex:** The Hithpael *ve-hithalakhti be-tokheikhem* ("I will walk about among you") is the most intimate divine-presence language in the covenant. The same form appears at Gen 3:8 (God walking in Eden), Gen 5:22 (Enoch walking with God), and Gen 6:9 (Noah walking with God). All of Leviticus — its blood offerings, purity regulations, calendar prescriptions, and ethical demands — is structural support for this single promise. The consensus preserves "I will walk among you" (following LOGOS/PNEUMA) over EMET's more emphatic "I will walk about in your midst" — though EMET's "about" captures the Hithpael's iterative force. The verse is cited in Ezek 37:27, Rev 21:3, and John 1:14 (*eskēnōsen*, "pitched his tent among us").

**On the sevenfold formula (vv. 18, 21, 24, 28):** The four occurrences of *sheva al-chattoteikhem* ("sevenfold for your sins") mark the escalating stages of covenant curse. Seven as the number of completeness means the full, complete measure of covenant consequence — not arithmetic multiplication but totality. The consensus renders "sevenfold" consistently throughout all four occurrences.

**On qeri as the chapter's legal spine:** The term *qeri* (and its prepositional phrases *be-qeri*, *imi be-qeri*) appears seven times in the chapter (vv. 21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 40, 41), forming a verbal pattern that mirrors the sevenfold curse. The chapter uses "walk contrary to me" / "walk contrary to them" to describe the bilateral breakdown of the covenant relationship — YHWH mirrors Israel's contrariness. The consensus adopts "contrary" throughout all seven occurrences.

**On the land's Sabbaths (vv. 34–35, 43):** The theology of the land keeping Sabbath in exile is unique in the Torah. The land is portrayed as a living moral subject with its own covenant rights: denied Sabbath rest by Israel's continuous cultivation, it receives its enforced rest through the desolation of exile. This is the explanation given in 2 Chr 36:21 for the seventy years of Babylonian exile. The *ratzah* verb ("be pleased with / enjoy") applied to the land enjoying its Sabbaths creates an ironic inversion: the land "enjoys" what human sin had denied it.

**On the reverse patriarchal order (v. 42):** The reversed sequence Jacob–Isaac–Abraham is preserved faithfully in both MT and LXX. It is a deliberate rhetorical movement from the most recent covenant partner back to the most foundational — ending with Abraham, whose covenant (Gen 15) was unconditionally one-sided (God alone walked through the cut animals). Even exile cannot reach back to undo the Abrahamic promise. The regression through the patriarchs is a movement from the complicated conditional history back to the pure unconditional foundation.

**On verse 44 as the chapter's theological floor:** After twenty-five verses of escalating catastrophe, the double negative of v. 44 — *lo ma'astim ve-lo ge'altim le-khalotam* ("I did not spurn them and I did not abhor them to destroy them completely") — establishes the covenant's irreversible floor. The exilic prophets (Isa 40–55; Lam 3:31; Jer 31:31–34) draw explicitly on this promise. The LXX's *ou mē bdelychthō* (strongest Greek negation form) is if anything more emphatic than the MT in insisting the floor holds.

**On LXX variants of note:**
- V. 9: LXX adds *epiblepsō eph' hymas* ("I will look upon you") before "make you fruitful" — a plus not in the MT.
- V. 30: LXX renders both *chamanim* and *gilulim* as *eidola* ("idols"), collapsing the MT's two distinct cult-object terms.
- V. 34: LXX's *eudokēsei* ("shall be pleased") is more explicit than the MT's *ratzah* in characterizing the land's Sabbath satisfaction.
- V. 42: LXX preserves the reversed patriarchal order and makes "I will remember the land" a separate clause.
- V. 46: LXX renders *ton nomon* (singular) for the final *torot* (plural), possibly reflecting a theological tendency to unify the Mosaic law under singular *nomos*.

---

*SOPHIA consensus completed. Leviticus 26:1–46. All 46 verses rendered. Source arbiter rulings logged in `disagreements/LEV_026.md`.*
